titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to Étienne Dumont, July 29, 1819
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date: June 30, 2026

respStmt.orgName: Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of the Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msIdentifier.repository: Bibliothèques municipales de la Ville de Genève

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to Étienne Dumont, , July 29th 1819

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
Your most touching and affectionate letter
reached me long since, and had I had any
thing new or interesting public or private to
communicate to you my dear and excellent
friend should have sooner heard from me: but
as you so justly say the recollections and
thoughts that we have in common with
each other do not depend upon the passing
moment for their interest. Though no letter
from me has told you so I hope you must be
certain that I thought of you and of the when
I lately read Mc.Intosh's admirable speech
on the penal laws of England. I wished to
have been near you that I might have enjoy-
edenjoyed the sight of the exquisite pleasure which
I know that triumph of reason and eloquence
must have given you - both as it regards
the living - and the dead. There cannot
be a finer tribute to the memory of him who
first began who so long so steadily carried
on that attack upon antient prejudice - And
now that the finest body of evidence, has been
produced, upon the largest scale which Time
and numbers can afford in the support of
truth it will be impossible in future to recur
to the old evasion of - " This is all very fine in
theory, but it will not do in practice. "
131
I do not recollect any instance in these or former
times of so large a view of legislative truths
being presented to the mind by any statesman much less by any orator
- nor was the subject ever before so successfully
treated as an experimental science. The expe-
rienceexperience of many ages was drawn into one
to one point and from the focus of his elo-
quenceeloquence the light and warmth spread far
and wide - to the eye and to the heart.

I own I never had such a high opinion as
you always expressed of Mc.Intosh till I read
that speech - and another of his on the Brazil
war. - I am heartily glad that the review
of your last volume Sur les peines & les recom-
-pensesrecompenses is still to be written. Those who have
read that book or the essays on legislation
must now look back to them with new
satisfaction - the satisfaction of being able
to support by notorious facts, the truth of great &
useful principles - All who cannot under-
standunderstand , or who will not attend to demonstra-
-tiondemonstration & who affect to despise theory, are compelled
to listen to evidence & are charmed to have a fact
to repeat in writing or conversation because
this carries with it the glory of authority -

I certainly was deeply interested when I first
read those books, and so was my dear father
- But I did not think that interest could now
have been so warmly received as I feel it has
been - There is no danger my dear friend that
this should exhaust itself and cool before I
can put it to any good use - I know that at least
where my friends & truth are concerned I am firm of purpose
- that much of my father still remains with me.

Before this reaches you I think I shall
have his memoirs nearly ready for the press.
- The first volume is half printed but the printing
goes on slowly because I have the proof sheets
sent to me here - I find it a great advantage - not
merely to verbal correction - I hope you will see
that I have improved what I had written by re-
-visionrevision after letting it lie by 6 months. All
the corrections and alterations have been
made on the general principles you pointed
out to me. I have not taken away any thing
that could interest the public for him, but
I have cut away much of the expression of my
own feelings in which the public could not
sympathise - I have taken away all direct praise
- all repetitions (I hope) And compression has given
strength - I hope I have also infused into it some
life which I felt that it wanted & which at the
time I wrote it first I had not the power to give.
- About Xmass it will be printed - I hope it will
not be published till spring - because I should
like to be abroad when it comes out.

I am not yet able to determine more
than I have stated in a note I have just written
to Pictet to which I refer you to avoid tiresome
repetitions - Upon second thoughts I recollect that
Pictet will not probably receive the note for some weeks
perhaps months after this letter may reach you. I have
sent it by a gentleman who takes this to France &
who has directions to forward it by the post; but he
will keep the note to Pictet which is a note of intro
duction till he reaches Geneva himself & he means to
make some stay at Paris. This gentleman & his wife
132
Mr. and Mrs. Lucas are friends of my friends but not
personally known to me - therefore I would not
though they wished it give them any introduction
to you - Your time is too precious & your mind engaged
too deeply to be squandered on every traveller who
may wish to have the honor of seeing you - For
the same reason I would not give an introduc-
tionintroduction to Mrs. Marcet. But I could not refuse one
to Pictet - I know they are people he would like &
that no one can be better suited to please them &
to do the honors of his own country to them &
so my conscience is clear & your time your own

- Give my affectionate & grateful love & Fannys
to Mrs. Marcet - She knows & onlywe know how
very much we have cause to bless her for her
kindness to us in London. When you read
this to her; she will say "No really I did nothing
at all for them I don't know what Miss E. means"

And you will not believe her - Tell her
that Fanny is well and perfectly happy and
decided in her determination - And I hope to
have the delight next year of presenting her
to Mrs. Marcet at Geneva in full bloom of health
and happiness. - I will write to Mrs. Marcet & when
I have received the work which she has been so kind
to send me - of which I expect the arrival every
day - As you are together it is better not to give
you two letters at once.

Honora is still with her two good aunts in England
they will bring her back to us this autumn.
Though you do not know these aunts, you know
I believe that we are much attached to them &
that they lived with us for 25 years part during
our happiest days; You will therefore rejoice
with us that they are coming back to live with
us again after having spent two years with other
friends in England. If my father could look see this
what pleasure it would give him to see the con-
tinuedcontinued union of all those whom he united
in esteem & affection. Besides he would feel
it as the strongest testimony to the kindness of conduct &
superior character of the wife he loved - the
sisters of a former wife returning after he is no
more to live with her & her children - To my
eldest brother his representative it is also hono-
rablehonorable - And - I am very absurd & unconsciona-
bleunconscionable to expect you to sympathise in all this - Pardon
and tell me any thing in revenge that con-
cernsconcerns your private family affairs -

Lovell (my eldest brother) has set an ex-
cellentexcellent example in this part of the country this
year as sheriff: by doing all that was formerly
done by military force, by the civil power only -
- he walked himself with constables, without soldiers
to conduct the prisoners through an immense
crowd in the town of Longford to and from the
jail - and when he as sheriff executed his
dreadful duty of attending the executions of
the condemned, he attended without any of
the military, with - There was perfect tranquillity
- and aweful silence - very unusual in such
cases in Ireland - ten or twelve thousand people
assembled - The solemnity of these executions & the
impression made by the example were strik-
ingstriking - in contrast to the too common & shocking
sacrifices & waste of human life seen often with indiffer-
enceindifference in this country or with a spirit of ven-
vengeance by surviving accomplices & relations

The silence of the crowd who followed one
of the prisoners when my brother was conducting
him back to the jail after sentence of death had
been pronounced, was once interrupted by deep sobs
133
- My brother looked to see from whence they came &
found saw a little boy of ten or eleven years old
who was struggling through the crowd to get to
his father the condemned prisoner. The boy as
all the neighbors who knew him attested was of
an excellent boy - Lovell after the execution of the
father who was condemned for stealing a horse,
brought the son home with him to Edgeworth Town
& put him to a school he has established for the
sons of the poor. The school contains above a hun-
dredhundred boys from 7 - to 12 years old - They one &
all received the poor little ragged creature, who
was brought amongst them with kindness - never
alluded to the disgrace of his father's having been
hanged - Lovell had desired that they should not
call him by his fathers name - but by his own
christian name Barney. They never failed to
do so - The boys all joined to earn a suit of clothes
for him - They said each earned the sum of sixpence
by working out of school hours, pounding stones
for the roads &c till they had made up the neces-
sarynecessary sum - One little creature of 8 years old
got up at 3 o clock in the morning to work for
his share - When the clothes were bought & made
they were carried by the boys, who hung them on the top of a pole
one of them a little fifer playing an Irish tune
to Barney - When he received them he burst
into tears and said

p.hi: I hope I will never do any thing bad

div.p:
This I warrant to you for plain truth
Indeed you will feel that it is too good for
fiction - For a person of good taste it is impossi-
bleimpossible to make any thing " plus belle que la Verite"

Now I will tell you something too bad
for fiction. - She would hardly dare to in
-ventinvent such a circumstance and expect
to be believed. I had it from One of the Judges who dined
with us this a few days ago. He told me that
as an instance that a year or two ago when
he was was going circuit as a judge in this
country, he inquired as it is the duty of
a judge to do whether there were any prison
ersprisoners still confined in the county Town at
which he had held his assize - Among others
he found one prisoner who had been con-
finedconfined in prison nine months - for having stolen
or rather on suspicion of having in a drun-
-kendrunken dispute carried off another man's hat.
- The loss & the suspicion of theft were sworn
to & the value of the hat mercifully laid at
under 40 shs. else it might have been a hang
inghanging matter - You will easily guess it was
an affair of party - between Catholic & Protes-
-tantProtestant - Croppy & Orangeman - Whether the
man accused of hat stealing - or his accuser was
Croppy or orangeman I forget.

Mr. Hunt who seemed to have deser
vedlydeservedly lost all favor even with the lowest
of the people of the London mob has as you
will see by the London papers (if they reach
you) regained his influence - He was harangu
-ingharanguing last week a mob of many thousands at Smith
field upon, It is curious that this modern
Jack Cade is haranguing on the spot where
his predecessor assembled a great multitude but
where such a multitude has never since
been assembled except at the great burning
match which our bloody queen Mary
held there - She made fire and
faggots blaze to burn protestants - and now
the cry of the modern Jack Cade is in favor
of Catholics! - I fear they will much hurt
the cause they pretend to serve - It requires
more discretion to be a good friend, than a
good enemy.

You will think perhaps that I am grow-
inggrowing a great politician - not at all. I have naturally
I mean habitually no taste even for reading the
newspapers; but my father strongly recommend
-edrecommended it to me to read them constantly that I might
keep up some knowledge of what is going on in the
world of realities & not live in a world of imagi-
nationimagination of my own - more agreeable but more
dangerous. Fortunately for me my sister Fanny
has really a genuine taste for politics & reads
the newspapers & all the parliamentary debates
with avidity very uncommon for so young
so pretty and so unpretending a person
- Now I benefit by this in two or three ways, she
keeps alive my interest, - saves my eyes - and
my understanding the labor of sieving the
sense from the nonsense - How I shall delight
in making Fanny known to you - She remem
bersremembers with a feeling of gratitude the kind manner
in which you spoke to her mother when she
met you but for a very short time two years ago
in London - We are reading together at a snails
pace Ricardo's political economy - There is
some puzzle in his book about the rent of land
which I think I shall never understand till
Mrs. Marcet explains it to me - Where does he
find that Number 1 of his divisions of land
cultivated which pays no rent - By the sup
positionsupposition I grant it to all his problems & it seems
necessary to all his demonstrations, but on
this habitable globe or in all the parts I know
or have heard of I should seek it in vain -

It is glorious for one man individual to have
enlightened a whole nation as Ricardo did, about
the Bullion question.

Lord Byron has a different taste for glory - or
rather for notoriety - His Mazeppa does not seem
to have pleased the London world much The day
after it
was published Lord Lansdowne wrote to me & he
concluded with "Lord Byrons readers are
surprised, I must not say disappointed to
find that in Mazeppa is neither satire scandal nor
licentiousness - but some pretty descriptions
tacked to a strange story" - I have not heard a
better description of it - The argument of the poem
is given in ten lines of Voltaire's inimitable
prose - from his history of Charles 12th.

Another poem of Lord Byron's Don Juan has
just appeared. It is said that it is not fit to ap-
-pearappear before any lady; whether this be the puff abu-
siveabusive or not I cannot say. It has not yet reached
ultimate Ireland. I am told that Murray found
it so abusive that he refused to publish it. The
present publisher whose name I forget objected
to many passages & a long correspondance has
been going on between the author & the friends
- there were some lines against Lord Castlereagh
which Lord B was prevailed upon to leave out
by his friends representing that if he published
them he must return to England to be ready
to answer a challenge which Lord C could not
fail to send him. So his hatred to England (- plus
a duel) proved greater than his hatred to Lord C -

Some attack on the Prince Regent has been
suppressed - and poor Southey has been some-
howsomehow or other snatched from his claws - But
there was one object on which he had fastened
his talons, from which he would not be terri-
fiedterrified even though he knew he must perish
in the attempt to wreak his vengeance there-

- He had written some bitter lines on Sir Samuel
Romilly
- In reply to the publisher & to Mr. Hobhouse's
entreaties that he would leave them out Lord By-
ron refused - saying that alive or dead he detested Sir
Samuel Romilly & would not relinquish his vengeance
135
- Lord Hobhouse has at his own peril taken them
out. - He has done well for Lord Byron his friend- As to our
friend Lord Byron could not touch his fame
There is in his reputation a living principle
of truth which the animals that prey upon the
dead cannot touch

My dear friend I must now stop - which
I ought to have done long ago - This is the longest
letter I have written these 6 months and I feel
pains in my eyes that tell me I must not do
so any more - I have, - what I am told is a
nervous disease in my eyes - violent pains in the
balls whenever they are tired -

Mrs. Edgeworth desires to be most affection-
atelyaffectionately remembered to you
div.closer:
I am your obliged & grateful friend
Maria Edgeworth.
